entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA rpoBacklog and supply commitments grow under 10% while Data Center revenue keeps rising, or leadTimes fall below 26 weeks, for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA's data-center sales jumped and its next-quarter guidance rose while package and memory lead times stretched past a year, so buyers keep ordering more than the company can ship.
entries.sensitivity: One soft quarter of data-center revenue, or lead times collapsing toward 26 weeks, would move this toward reaffirmed or weakened.
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.title: NVDA demand durability

entries.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS-S/L leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM3E returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
entries.mechanism: Memory shortages stretched NVIDIA's advanced-package lead times past a year, so packaging and memory, not wafers or orders, decide how much GPU revenue can ship.
entries.sensitivity: If the simultaneous next-generation ramps cut lead times below 26 weeks, this weakens quickly.
entries.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
entries.title: Supply constraint binding

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue growth falls below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler MI-series deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
entries.mechanism: AMD's data-center revenue grew 57% year over year with named OpenAI and Oracle deployments and a rising next-generation roadmap, so it is winning real accelerator volume rather than only sampling.
entries.sensitivity: A stall in Instinct share below 6% or loss of the OpenAI ramp would weaken this.
entries.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
entries.title: AMD credible second source

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue (D2) growth stays above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
entries.mechanism: Custom accelerators stayed inside their owners' clouds with no rental market while NVIDIA's data-center revenue reaccelerated and GPUs held about 60% of one large cloud's build, so in-house chips add capacity rather than displace GPU demand.
entries.sensitivity: External rental of a custom ASIC at scale, or NVIDIA growth stalling, would re-strengthen it.
entries.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
entries.title: Custom ASIC substitution

entries.falsifiableTrigger: D6 B200/H100 median dollar-per-GPU-hour declines at least 15% at two or more tracked providers within one quarter, or H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Rental rates for H100 and B200 stayed well above floor levels and NVIDIA held a 75% gross margin, so buyers still pay premium prices even as new capacity arrives.
entries.sensitivity: A sharp rental-price cut across providers as 2H26 supply lands would weaken this.
entries.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
entries.title: Pricing power persistence

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200/advanced-compute China case-by-case licensing and the 25% Section-232 tariff, and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Washington shifted H200-class China sales from denial to case-by-case review, so the policy now constrains a smaller share of demand than it did under a full ban.
entries.sensitivity: Full license removal plus booked China revenue would break it, while a re-tightening of controls would re-strengthen it.
entries.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
entries.title: Export control exposure

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q discloses customer financing/notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed AI-cloud/neocloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
entries.mechanism: If NVIDIA underwrote purchases via credit or revenue-share, reported demand would partly reflect NVIDIA-funded orders, creating a loop exposed to customer default — but observed demand is customer-capex-funded.

pendingChallenge.rationale: NVIDIA introduced a revenue-sharing and credit-support program that helps AI clouds buy its infrastructure, which is the exact financing channel the thesis flags, so more reported demand now rests on credit NVIDIA extends.

entries.sensitivity: Disclosure of financing receivables and the financial health of concentrated neocloud/hyperscaler customers.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
entries.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per 10-K/10-Q customerConcentration disclosure.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA's largest direct customer grew to 22% of revenue and its second to 14%, up from about 11% each a year earlier, so a growing share of sales rides on a few buyers.
entries.sensitivity: Broadening of the customer base below a 40% top-three share would weaken this.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
entries.title: Customer concentration risk

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching by selling Spectrum-X and Quantum together with Blackwell and GB300, so buyers who adopt its racks find it costlier to mix in rival accelerators.
entries.sensitivity: Networking revenue growing slower than compute, or losing the switch-market lead, would weaken it.
entries.statement: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
entries.title: Networking attach as systems moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue (D2) growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Each NVIDIA generation sharply lowers the cost of generating a token, so workloads that were too expensive become affordable and total GPU consumption rises.
entries.sensitivity: A generation that fails to lower cost per token, or inference demand rolling over, would undercut it.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
entries.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand
